abstract.para: Resource constraints let you specify which nodes resources can run on, what order resources should start in, and which resources are dependent on one another.

important.title: Restrictions for constraints and primitive resources

listitem.para: Don't add colocation constraints to primitives in a resource group. Add a colocation constraint to the group instead.
Don't add any constraints to a primitive that has been cloned. Add constraints to the clone instead.

section.title: Scores and kinds
section.para: Location and colocation constraints must have a score to indicate how important the constraint is. A score of INFINITY (positive or negative) means that the constraint must be applied. The score can also be a non-infinite number (positive or negative), which means the constraint is only a preference. In this case, the constraint might not be applied if its score is outweighed by other factors, such as other constraints, resource stickiness, failure thresholds, etc.

For location constraints, a positive score means the resource should (or must) run on the specified node. A negative score means the resource should not (or must not) run on the specified node. You can also add multiple location constraints with different scores to create an order of node preference.

For colocation constraints, a positive score means the resources should (or must) run together on the same node. A negative score means the resources should not (or must not) run together on the same node.
Instead of a score, order constraints have a kind, which defines whether the constraint is mandatory or optional.

section.title: Resource sets
section.para: Resource sets let you group multiple resources in a single constraint instead of needing multiple constraints. The effect depends on the type of constraint:

listitem.para: In a location constraint, a resource set applies the constraint to multiple resources instead of just one, without creating any dependency between the resources.

In an order constraint, a resource set gives multiple resources the same position in the order. For example, a simple order constraint says that res1 must start first, then res2, then res3. However, if you put res2 and res3 in a resource set, the constraint would say that res1 must start first, and then res2 and res3 can start in any order.

In a colocation constraint, a resource set gives multiple resources the same relationship to the next resource. For example, a simple colocation constraint says that res1 must run on the same node as res2, which must run on the same node as res3. However, if you put res1 and res2 in a resource set, the constraint would say that res1 and res2 must run on the same node as res3, but aren't otherwise dependent on each other.

section.para: The syntax for resource sets can be complex, so before using them in a production environment we recommend testing them to make sure the resources behave as expected.
